one_line_takeaway: A production, cryptographically-attested admission and per-server tool-allowlist layer bolted above MCP (no protocol messages changed) that a large hermetic test suite plus a 27,025-input LLM-generated adversarial corpus show reliably denies forged clearance assertions and out-of-allowlist tool calls with zero leaked network writes -- though it explicitly does not address post-admission schema drift or misbehavior by an already-admitted, correctly-cleared server.
key_finding: All 48 hermetic tests pass (44 under vitest, 4 under node:test), including 20 adversarial tests: for tool-name least-privilege, 31 evasions were all denied (mcp.tool.deny) with 0 network writes, only the 2 exact allowlisted names admitted; for authenticated/authorized admission, 2 valid assertions were admitted (including a host-bound assertion from its own origin) and all 12 invalid variants were denied, each at its expected guard clause. The LLM-driven coverage campaign (llama3.2 via Ollama) generated 47,945 tool-name evasions, of which 27,025 were unique, and every one was denied with zero network writes and zero admitted (breakdown by category: 12,106 unclassified/other, 9,012 whitespace/control-char, 3,469 separator/command-chaining, 1,251 near-miss edit-distance <=2, 752 path-traversal, 393 homoglyph/zero-width/RTL, 42 case-variant). Of the model's forged clearance assertions, 14,378 distinct ones survived JSON parsing and all 14,378 were rejected (0 admitted). The live Gmail end-to-end test against the real endpoint admitted the allowlisted tool the model selected with no admission denial, and denied the out-of-allowlist tool before any network call. The authors report the adversarial harness surfaced a genuine origin-binding weakness in an earlier version of the verifier, which is why the shipped verifier now enforces the signed host allow-list (Property 1 clause h) -- i.e., the red-teaming process itself drove a real fix rather than only re-confirming a pre-decided design.
core_contribution: The paper documents mcp-attested, a shipping additive security extension to the Model Context Protocol (MCP) called Attested Tool-Server Admission (ATSA), and generalizes it into a normative (RFC-2119-style) standardization proposal. It contributes: (1) a clearance assertion format -- a small, offline-signed JSON document (the Server Attestation Document / SAD) published by an MCP server at a well-known URI (/.well-known/mcp-attestation, or /.well-known/enclawed-clearance.json in the Enclawed profile) that binds server identity to a sensitivity clearance verifiable against a host-local pinned trust root, reusing the same Ed25519 canonical-body signing flow already used for Enclawed extension manifests; (2) a host-side admission algorithm that verifies capability, signedness, signer authority/expiry, signature, clearance domination, and origin binding, in a fixed order, before any tool dispatch, plus a deny-by-default per-server closed tool allowlist that is enforced independently of what the server's tools/list advertises; (3) a flavor-gated enforcement model (open flavor = warn-but-allow, enclaved flavor = hard deny) with every admission/tool decision appended to a hash-chained, tamper-evident audit log; and (4) an adversarial evaluation combining a hand-written mutation-testing suite with an LLM-generated red-team corpus. The mechanism adds no new MCP message type and is designed to be adoptable as an optional MCP addendum.
threat_model: Deployment: one operator/organization per Enclawed gateway instance; the host and its locally pinned trust root are part of the trusted computing base; the LLM itself is NOT trusted -- the agent's instruction stream is assumed partially attacker-controlled via retrieved content, tool outputs, and message bodies (standard prompt-injection posture). Adversary capabilities: (1) inject instructions steering the model toward an attacker-chosen MCP server or tool; (2) stand up a network MCP server with a valid TLS cert for a domain the attacker controls; (3) present a self-declared tool list and self-declared sensitivity level; (4) replay or tamper with a legitimately-issued clearance assertion in transit. Adversary limits: cannot forge an Ed25519 signature without a trust-root private key; cannot mutate the pinned trust root (locked after startup); is not a co-tenant on the host in the base model -- multi-tenant adversarial isolation on one shared gateway is explicitly out of scope. Stated goals: G1 (never enforce admission of a server whose clearance assertion is missing/malformed/unsigned/untrusted-signer/expired-signer/unapproved-level/bad-signature/below-required-level/off-origin), G2 (never dispatch a tool the operator did not pre-authorize for that server, regardless of server advertisement or model request), G3 (every admission decision recorded in tamper-evident storage), G4 (pinned trust root cannot be mutated at runtime). The paper is explicit that it does NOT defend against a correctly-admitted server misbehaving within its granted tools (that is capability containment, a separate cited paper), content inspection of data crossing an admitted connection (egress reference monitor, separate cited paper), a model refusing to use a tool, or multi-tenant isolation on a shared gateway.
limitations: Stated by the authors: (1) clearance binds to a server as a whole, not to individual tools, so a server cannot expose a PUBLIC tool alongside a RESTRICTED tool at different levels -- flagged as future work (per-tool clearance); (2) beyond per-signer notAfter, there is no per-assertion freshness (notBefore/expiry) or online revocation channel (e.g., OCSP-style) for compromised signer keys; (3) the normative extension (Section 8.1) is specified but not yet submitted to the MCP maintainers, and reconciling the clearance field with MCP's evolving OAuth profile/capability-negotiation handshake remains open; (4) the extension explicitly does not protect against a correctly-admitted server misbehaving within its granted tools, does not inspect content crossing an admitted connection, does not address an LLM simply refusing to use a tool, and does not provide multi-tenant adversarial isolation on a shared gateway; (5) does not address post-admission schema/behavior drift of an already-admitted server -- explicitly deferred to a separate downstream 'runtime drift monitoring' layer described only at a design/interface level (Section 9), not implemented or evaluated in this paper; (6) the trust-root integrity itself rests on trusting the host binary (a TCB assumption, not a property this layer proves); (7) the defender-side AlgoVoi Agent-Trust-Bench baseline data cited in Sections 3 and 7 is an external, cited third-party benchmark (not the paper's own experiment) used to motivate the threat taxonomy, and should not be read as this paper's own measured result.

datasets_used: No conventional ML dataset; the evaluation inputs are an LLM-generated adversarial text/JSON corpus: hand-written mutation test vectors (Section 7.1/Appendix D, 11 conformance vectors) and a large llama3.2-generated corpus of tool-name evasion strings and forged clearance-assertion JSON documents (Section 7.3)
quantitative_results: 31/31 tool-name evasions denied (mcp.tool.deny), 0 network writes, only 2 allowlisted names admitted
2/2 valid clearance assertions admitted; 12/12 invalid assertion variants denied at expected guard clause
LLM coverage campaign: 47,945 tool-name evasions generated, 27,025 unique, all 27,025 denied, 0 admitted, 0 network writes

Tool-name evasion category breakdown (unique/denied): unclassified/other 12,106; whitespace/control-char 9,012; separator/command-chaining 3,469; near-miss (edit distance <=2) 1,251; path-traversal 752; homoglyph/zero-width/RTL 393; case-variant 42
Forged clearance assertions: 14,378 unique (survived JSON parsing), all 14,378 rejected, 0 admitted
Live Gmail MCP endpoint test: allowlisted tool admitted and dispatched with no admission denial; out-of-allowlist tool denied before any network call
Campaign config: Ollama 0.24.0, llama3.2 (3B), RTX 4090 24GB GPU, NUM_PARALLEL=6, ctx 4096, 1500 generation rounds per phase
baselines_compared: No ML-model baselines are compared; this is a systems/mechanism paper. The paper cites (but does not itself run) an external third-party defender-side benchmark, AlgoVoi's Agent-Trust-Bench (Hopley & AlgoVoi, ref [8]), described as showing that relying on the LLM layer alone (via personas) to detect tool-name shadowing and schema drift is brittle and degrades under context exhaustion -- used only as motivating context in Section 3/7.3, not as a comparison arm the authors evaluated themselves
